title: Bash: Building A Boilerplate - Quiz
description: Test your understanding of terminal basics, file system navigation, and file management commands

questions.question: What does the `pwd` command display?

options.text: The present working directory (full absolute path)
The password of the current user

questions.explanation: The 'pwd' command stands for 'print working directory'. It outputs the full absolute path of the current directory you are in, such as /home/user/projects.

questions.question: What does `mkdir -p parent/child/grandchild` do?

options.text: Creates only the 'grandchild' directory and errors if parent directories don't exist
Creates the entire directory path, creating parent directories as needed
Creates a directory called 'parent/child/grandchild' with slashes in the name
Creates three separate directories named 'parent', 'child', and 'grandchild'

questions.explanation: The -p (--parents) flag tells mkdir to create the entire directory path, including any intermediate directories that don't yet exist. Without -p, mkdir would fail if 'parent' or 'parent/child' didn't exist.

questions.question: What does the `touch` command do?

options.text: Creates an empty file or updates the timestamp of an existing file
Displays the contents of a file

questions.explanation: The 'touch' command creates a new empty file if it doesn't exist, or updates the access and modification timestamps of an existing file without changing its content.

questions.question: What does `cd ..` do?

options.text: Changes to the parent directory (one level up)
Changes to the previous directory you were in

questions.explanation: The '..' refers to the parent directory. 'cd ..' moves you one level up in the directory hierarchy. A single dot '.' refers to the current directory, and 'cd -' takes you to the previous directory.

questions.question: Which command copies a file called 'index.html' to a directory called 'backup'?
questions.explanation: The 'cp' command copies files or directories. 'cp index.html backup/' copies the file into the backup directory. 'mv' would move (not copy) the file. 'copy' and 'duplicate' are not standard Linux commands.

questions.question: What does `ls -a` show that `ls` alone does not?

options.text: Hidden files and directories (those starting with a dot)

questions.explanation: The -a (--all) flag shows all entries including hidden files and directories that start with a dot (.), such as .gitignore or .bashrc. The -l flag shows permissions and details, not -a.

questions.question: What does `rm -r directory_name` do?

options.text: Removes the directory and all its contents recursively

questions.explanation: The rm command with the -r (--recursive) flag removes a directory and all of its contents, including subdirectories and files. Without -r, rm cannot remove directories. Use with caution as this is irreversible.

questions.question: What does the `mv` command do when given a file and a new filename?

options.text: Creates a copy of the file with the new name
Renames the file to the new name
Creates a symbolic link with the new name
Merges the file with another file of the new name

questions.explanation: The 'mv' command serves two purposes: moving files to a different location, and renaming files. When given 'mv oldname newname' in the same directory, it renames the file. It does not create a copy.

questions.question: How do you access the manual page for the `ls` command?
questions.explanation: The 'man' (manual) command displays the manual page for a given command. 'man ls' shows detailed documentation for ls including all available flags and options. Press 'q' to exit the manual.

questions.question: What does `cd` with no arguments do?

options.text: Changes to the user's home directory

questions.explanation: Running 'cd' with no arguments changes the current directory to the user's home directory (equivalent to 'cd ~' or 'cd $HOME'). This is a quick way to return to your home directory from anywhere in the file system.
